confidence.disclosure: Itinerary is built from the Liujiagang and Changle stone inscriptions (commissioned by Zheng He himself in 1431), the Ming Shilu, and the eyewitness accounts of his interpreters Ma Huan (Yingya Shenglan) and Fei Xin (Xingcha Shenglan), as analysed in Edward Dreyer's Zheng He: China and the Oceans (2007). Per the brief, only 4-5 key ports per voyage are listed; some ports were visited by detached squadrons rather than by Zheng He personally — these are noted in the event descriptions where relevant.

sources: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)
Edward L. Dreyer, Zheng He: China and the Oceans in the Early Ming Dynasty 1405-1433 (2007)

journey.event: Born Ma He into a Muslim family in Yunnan, then under the Mongol Yuan dynasty; his father and grandfather had both made the Hajj.
Captured by Ming forces at age 10, castrated, and conscripted into the household of Zhu Di, the future Yongle Emperor.
Granted the surname Zheng by the new Yongle Emperor for his role in the civil war and made head eunuch of the imperial household.
First voyage departs from Liujiagang on the Yangtze with 317 ships and over 27,000 men.
First voyage's first major port of call: the Cham capital of Vijaya.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: Visited Majapahit-controlled Java; brief skirmish in which 170 Chinese sailors were killed.
First voyage reached Calicut, the great Malabar pepper port; defeated the pirate Chen Zuyi at Palembang on the return.
Second voyage stopped at Malacca, helping cement Ming patronage of the rising sultanate.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: Second voyage reached Calicut and returned to China.
Third voyage erected the trilingual Galle Stele in Chinese, Tamil and Persian, honouring Buddhist, Hindu and Muslim deities.
Captured King Alagakkonara of Ceylon after a confrontation, taking him as prisoner to Nanjing.
Fourth voyage assembled at the northern Sumatran sultanate of Samudera Pasai, the staging point for the Indian Ocean crossing.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: Visited the Maldive islands during the fourth voyage, which extended Ming reach beyond India.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: First Chinese fleet to reach the Persian Gulf, anchoring at the great spice and pearl entrepôt of Hormuz.
Returned to China with envoys from 19 states; the gift of a Bengali giraffe was hailed as a qilin.
Fifth voyage departed from Quanzhou; Zheng He erected a stone tablet commemorating his prayers for divine protection on 31 May 1417.
Reached Aden during the fifth voyage; the Rasulid sultan presented giraffes, oryxes and other African animals.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: First Chinese fleet known to reach the East African coast; archaeological finds of Ming porcelain confirm the visit.
Reached Malindi, the southernmost confirmed point of the treasure-fleet voyages.
Returned from the fifth voyage with envoys from East Africa and the Arabian peninsula.
Sixth voyage departed in late 1421 to escort foreign envoys home.
Sixth voyage's principal Persian Gulf landfall; detached squadrons reached Brava and Mogadishu before returning.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: Seventh voyage assembled at Changle, where Zheng He erected the second stone tablet recording his earlier expeditions.
Seventh voyage reached Calicut, where it divided to send detachments to the Red Sea and East Africa.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: Detached squadron from Calicut sailed to Jeddah; seven of Ma Huan's companions then travelled overland to Mecca and Medina.
journey.source: Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan / The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (1433)

journey.event: Main fleet departed Hormuz on 9 March 1433 for the return voyage.
Zheng He died at sea or at Calicut in 1433 during the return leg of the seventh voyage.
A symbolic tomb was erected at Niu Shou Mountain near Nanjing; his actual burial place is not known.
